title: White bean and rosemary soup
subtitle: Italian-style cannellini bean soup with rosemary and garlic.
excerpt: Cannellini beans simmered with rosemary, garlic, and vegetable stock, then part-blended so half the beans are smooth and half remain whole. Finished with good olive oil. Five ingredients; deeply satisfying.
sourceNotes: Bean and rosemary soups are a fixture of Tuscan and Umbrian cooking. The combination of cannellini beans with rosemary (fagioli con rosmarino) appears throughout central Italian regional cookery. Marcella Hazan's Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking documents the Tuscan approach; the combination has become standard in British home cooking as tinned cannellini beans became a pantry staple.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to three months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in the fridge for four days. Add a splash of water when reheating.

content.text: This is a pantry soup: tinned cannellini beans, dried rosemary or a sprig from the garden, a few cloves of garlic, stock, and good olive oil to finish. Nothing else is needed. The part-blending technique gives the soup body while keeping enough whole beans for texture.

The olive oil at the end matters. A thin soup finished with water-based olive oil is flat; the same soup finished with cold-pressed extra-virgin oil from a good bottle is something entirely different. Use the best you have.

items.prepNote: two 400 g tins, drained and rinsed

content.text: A large saucepan and a stick blender. The recipe uses tinned beans; dried beans soaked overnight also work but add 90 minutes of cooking.

Warm {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and the rosemary sprigs. Let them sizzle very gently for 2–3 minutes until the garlic is just golden and fragrant. Add the chilli flakes if using. Do not let the garlic brown.
Add the drained cannellini beans and stir to coat in the garlic oil. Pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 20 minutes so the beans absorb the flavours.

Remove the rosemary sprigs. Use a stick blender to blend half the soup — dip it in and blend for 15–20 seconds, leaving the other half as whole beans. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Ladle into bowls and finish each with a generous drizzle of extra-virgin olive oil.

items.symptom: Garlic is bitter rather than sweet
items.cause: Garlic overcooked or burned in the oil.
items.fix: Burnt garlic cannot be rescued. Start again with fresh garlic, keeping the heat low and the cooking time to 2–3 minutes.

items.cause: Tinned beans have a neutral flavour and need the garlic-rosemary oil to work.
items.fix: Add a teaspoon of miso-white to the stock, or use a stock cube for additional depth.

items.cause: Cannellini beans blend to a very starchy purée.
items.fix: Add more hot stock or water.

items.cause: Too long in the oil, or very resinous rosemary.
items.fix: Remove the sprigs earlier next time. A single small sprig is enough if the rosemary is very aromatic.

content.text: This is Tuscan cucina povera: frugal and plainly made from what is in the larder. Fagioli all'uccelletto (beans with sage and garlic) and ribollita (the bread-thickened version) are the best-known relatives. The white bean and rosemary soup is simpler than either: no bread, no stewing, just the beans and the fragrant oil. It reached the British home kitchen through Italian cookery writers of the 1980s and 1990s and has barely changed since.
